subject: mathematics
chapter_title: Predicting What Comes Next: Exploring Sequences and Progressions

sections.title: Introduction to Sequences
sections.think_reflect: Asks the student to describe the pattern in each of the natural-number, odd-number, triangular-number, and square-number sequences and predict the next few numbers in each, and separately to list five different types of sequences and discuss their properties.

sections.title: Explicit Rule for a Sequence
sections.think_reflect: Asks why it is useful to have an explicit formula for the nth term of a sequence, and separately asks the student to find the rule describing the nth term of the sequence of square numbers.

sections.title: Arithmetic Progressions
sections.think_reflect: Asks the student to predict the number of squares at later and arbitrary stages of the growing square pattern, and separately to decide which of the chapter's sequences discussed so far are arithmetic progressions and justify the claim.

sections.title: Sum of the First n Natural Numbers
sections.think_reflect: Asks whether the reverse-and-add approach used for the sum of the first ten natural numbers can be extended to find the sum 1+2+3+...+100, whether the resulting formula can be used to find S20, S50, or S1000, and asks the student to use the triangular-number formula to find the 10th, 17th, and 80th triangular numbers.

sections.title: Geometric Progressions
sections.think_reflect: Asks the student to predict the number of green squares at later and arbitrary stages of the doubling square pattern and to explain how this growth differs from the arithmetic-progression pattern in section 8.4.

sections.title: Fun with Fractals
sections.think_reflect: Asks the student to count black triangles across Stages 0-3 of the Sierpinski triangle, predict the count at Stages 4 and 5, find a rule for the nth stage, and separately work out and explain the geometric-progression rule for the shrinking area of the black region at each stage.

prose_summary: Section 8.1 introduces sequences by defining a sequence as an ordered list of numbers in which each number is a term, illustrating with the familiar natural-number, odd-number, triangular-number, and square-number sequences, distinguishing finite from infinite sequences, and developing the triangular-number and square-number sequences as running sums of natural numbers and odd numbers respectively (with accompanying dot-array figures), before introducing the subscript notation t1, t2, t3, ... (and s, u as alternative letters for other sequences) to name terms by position, noting that the position index n is always a non-negative integer while the term value itself may be negative or any real number, and closing with an inline exercise on the sequence 1, 4, 7, 10, 13, ... and its running-sum sequence. Section 8.2 defines and explains the explicit rule for the nth term of a sequence, a formula that computes any term directly from its position number n without needing earlier terms, worked through Example 1 (un = 2n - 1 for odd numbers) and Example 2 (sn = 5n - 2), both examples also showing how the explicit rule is solved in reverse to test whether a given number is a term of the sequence and, if so, to find its position; the section closes with an inline exercise on tn = 3n - 7 asking for specific terms, the position of 332, and whether 557 is a term. Section 8.3 introduces the recursive rule, which defines each term in relation to one or more previous terms rather than via the position number directly, contrasted with the explicit rule using the sequence 1, 4, 7, 10, 13, ... (tn = tn-1 + 3), and worked through Example 3 (un = 2un-1 + 3) and Example 4 (sn = sn-1(sn-1 - 1)), both computing successive terms from the given starting value; the section then introduces the Virahanka-Fibonacci sequence as a recursive rule depending on the previous two terms (Vn = Vn-1 + Vn-2), narrates its discovery by Virahanka in the context of Prakrit poetic meter and its later study by Gopala, Hemachandra, and Fibonacci, and closes with Exercise Set 8.1 (six questions on explicit-rule term-finding, term verification, and a three-term recursive rule). Section 8.4 defines and explains the arithmetic progression (AP), introduced through a growing pattern of stages where four squares are added at each stage, producing the sequence 1, 5, 9, 13, ... ; the section derives the explicit rule tn = 1 + (n-1) x 4 = 4n - 3 for this pattern, identifies the constant difference between consecutive terms as the defining property of an AP, generalises to the standard form a, a+d, a+2d, ... with first term a and common difference d (positive or negative, illustrated with the decreasing AP 11, 7, 3, -1, -5, ...), and gives the general explicit rule tn = a + (n-1)d. Section 8.4.1 visualises an AP by tabulating stage number against the number of squares, plotting the resulting ordered pairs, and observing that they lie on a straight line, then states the recursive rule for an AP (t1 = a, tn = tn-1 + d for n >= 2) and closes with Example 5, which models a taxi fare with a Rs.200 booking fee plus Rs.40 per kilometre as the AP 240, 280, 320, ... and derives its nth term 200 + 40n. Section 8.5 derives the formula for the sum of the first n natural numbers: it first computes the sum of the first ten natural numbers by writing the sum forwards and backwards and pairing terms that each total 11, generalises this reverse-and-add argument algebraically to obtain Sn = n(n+1)/2, restates the same result pictorially via a dot diagram showing a rectangular array split by a zigzag partition, applies the formula to compute a sum of consecutive integers (25 to 58) by differencing two partial sums, attributes the earliest known statement of the result to Aryabhata's Aryabhatiya, and connects the formula back to the triangular-number sequence by showing that its nth term equals n(n+1)/2; the section closes with Exercise Set 8.2 (seven questions on AP terms, term position, recursive rules, a system of two linear equations for a and d, counting and summing multiples, and a salary-increment and marble-arrangement word problem). Section 8.6 defines and explains the geometric progression (GP), introduced through a growing pattern of stages where the number of green squares doubles at each stage, producing the sequence 3, 6, 12, 24, ... ; the section derives the explicit rule tn = 3 x 2^(n-1), identifies the constant ratio between consecutive terms (the common ratio) as the defining property of a GP, generalises to the standard form a, ar, ar^2, ... with first term a and common ratio r, gives the general explicit rule tn = ar^(n-1), and works through Example 6, Example 7, Example 8 (testing whether given sequences are GPs and finding their common ratios) and Example 9 (verifying that 5, 15/4, 45/16, 135/64, ... is a GP with r = 3/4 by computing three consecutive term ratios, then writing its nth term), closing with two inline exercises testing further sequences for the GP property and finding a recursive rule for tn = 3 x 10^(n-1). Section 8.6.1 explores the Sierpinski triangle fractal, constructed by repeatedly removing the central triangle from each black triangle to produce Stages 0, 1, 2, 3, ..., establishes that the number of black triangles (1, 3, 9, 27, ...) forms a GP with explicit rule 3^n and recursive rule t1 = 1, tn = 3 x tn-1, separately establishes that the shaded area of the black region forms a different GP with explicit rule (3/4)^n and recursive rule s1 = 1, sn = (3/4) x sn-1 (since each stage retains 3/4 of the previous stage's area), consolidates both rules in a summary table (Table 1), explains the general concept of a fractal as a self-similar pattern repeating at every scale and gives natural examples (tree branching, cauliflower/broccoli, snowflakes, coastlines), and introduces Wacław Sierpiński as the mathematician for whom the fractal is named. Section 8.6.2 visualises a GP by tabulating stage number against the number of green squares from Fig. 8.6, plotting the resulting ordered pairs and observing that, unlike an AP, they do not lie on a straight line but curve upward, similarly plots the Sierpinski-triangle black-triangle counts and shaded areas against stage number to show one curve rising rapidly and one curve falling toward zero, and closes with Example 10, which models a bouncing ball that retains 3/4 of its previous height on each bounce as a GP (a = 18, r = 0.75), computes the height after each of seven bounces, and determines that the ball falls below one-sixth of its drop height after the seventh bounce; the chapter then presents Exercise Set 8.3 (seven questions on GP terms, term position, a starred recursive-rule item, the explicit and recursive formulas for a GP, two bouncing-ball and fractal word problems) followed by an End-of-Chapter Exercise set of fifteen questions of which only Q1 and Q2 are unstarred (finding an AP term from two given terms, and determining an AP from its third term and a difference condition), and closes with a Chapter Summary restating the definitions of sequence, term, explicit formula, recursive formula, the triangular-number formula, the AP and its nth-term formula, and the GP and its nth-term formula, plus a closing note that fractals such as the Sierpinski triangle and Sierpinski square carpet give rise to geometric progressions.

enumerated_worked_examples.description: Shows that the expression un = 2n - 1 generates the sequence of odd numbers by substituting n = 1, 2, 3 to get u1 = 1, u2 = 3, u3 = 5, establishing un = 2n - 1 as the explicit rule for the odd-number sequence, then uses the rule in reverse to find that the odd number 137 is the 69th term (solving 2n - 1 = 137).

For the sequence generated by sn = 5n - 2, checks whether 308 and 471 are terms by solving 5n - 2 = 308 (giving n = 62, so 308 is the 62nd term) and 5n - 2 = 471 (giving n = 94.6, a non-natural number, so 471 is not a term).

Finds the first four terms of the recursively defined sequence u1 = 1, un = 2un-1 + 3 for n >= 2 by successive substitution, obtaining 1, 5, 13, 29, and notes that subsequent terms can be calculated to check whether 133 is a term.
Finds the first four terms of the recursively defined sequence s1 = 3, sn = sn-1(sn-1 - 1) for n >= 2 by successive substitution, obtaining 3, 6, 30, 870.

Models a taxi fare with a fixed booking fee of Rs.200 plus Rs.40 per kilometre as the AP 240, 280, 320, ... with first term 240 and common difference 40, and derives the nth term as 200 + 40n where n is the distance travelled in km.
Asks whether 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, ... is a geometric progression and, if so, what its common ratio is.
Asks whether 1, 3, 9, 27, 81, ... is a geometric progression and, if so, what its common ratio is.
Asks whether 1, -1, 1, -1, 1, ... is a geometric progression and, if so, what its common ratio is.
Verifies that the sequence 5, 15/4, 45/16, 135/64, ... is a geometric progression by computing three consecutive term ratios (each equal to 3/4), then writes its nth term as 5 x (3/4)^(n-1).

Models a ball dropped from 24 feet that rebounds to 3/4 of its previous height on each bounce as a GP with a = 18 and r = 0.75, computes the maximum height after each of the first seven bounces (18, 13.5, 10.125, 7.59375, 5.695, 4.27125, 3.2034375 feet), and determines that the ball first remains below one-sixth of the original drop height after the seventh bounce.

enumerated_exercises.description: For the sequence 1, 4, 7, 10, 13, ..., predict the next four terms, and derive the first 10 terms of the sequence formed by summing all terms up to a given term.
Write t5, t6, t7, and t8 for the sequence of triangular numbers.
Using the explicit rule un = 2n - 1, find the 53rd, 108th, and 1170th terms of the odd number sequence.

For the expression tn = 3n - 7: (i) find the first, second, third, 12th, 18th, and 50th terms; (ii) determine which term of the sequence is 332; (iii) determine whether 557 is a term of this sequence, with reasoning.

Verify that the sequences (i) 2, 5, 8, 11, ... and (ii) -5, -1, 3, 7, ... are arithmetic progressions, write their nth terms, and describe what is observed when the ordered pairs emerging from each are plotted.
Using the formula tn = a + (n-1) x d, find the nth term of the arithmetic progressions (i) 1/2, 5/2, 9/2, 13/2, ... and (ii) 1.5, 3.5, 5.5, 7.5, ...
Find recursive rules for the arithmetic progressions given in the previous two exercises.
Check whether the sequences (i) 2, 10, 50, 250, ..., (ii) 4, 8/3, 16/9, 32/27, ..., and (iii) 3, -3/2, 3/4, -3/8, ... are geometric progressions and find their nth terms.
Find a recursive rule for the formula tn = 3 x 10^(n-1) that generates the geometric progression 3, 30, 300, 3000, ...
Find the first five terms of the sequence in which the nth term is given by (i) tn = 3n - 4, (ii) tn = 2 - 5n, and (iii) tn = n^2 - 2n + 3 for n >= 1.
Find the 10th and 15th terms of the sequence tn = 5n - 3 for n >= 1.
Determine whether 97 and 172 are terms of the sequence tn = 5n - 3 for n >= 1.
Find which term of the sequence tn = 5n - 3 for n >= 1 equals 607.
For the sequence given by the recursive rule t1 = -5, tn+1 = tn + 3 for n >= 1, find the first five terms and determine whether 52 is a term, and if so, which term it is.
For the recursive rule T1 = 1, T2 = 2, T3 = 4, Tn = Tn-1 + Tn-2 + Tn-3 for n >= 4, find T4, T5, T6, T7, and T8.
Find the 10th and 26th terms of the AP 3, 8, 13, 18, ...
Determine which term of the AP 21, 18, 15, ... is -81, and determine whether 0 is a term of this AP, with reasons.
Find the nth term of the AP 11, 8, 5, 2, ... and write the recursive rule for this AP.
Given an AP of 50 terms in which the 3rd term is 12 and the last (50th) term is 106, find the 29th term by solving the pair of linear equations a + 2d = 12 and a + 49d = 106.
Find how many 2-digit numbers are divisible by 3 and the sum of all these 2-digit numbers.
Given a starting annual salary of Rs.5,00,000 with an annual increment of Rs.20,000, find after how many years the income reaches Rs.7,00,000.
Given a marble arrangement where row k has k marbles for 25 rows, find the total number of marbles used.
Find the 12th term of a GP with common ratio 2, given that its 8th term is 192.
Find the 10th and nth terms of the GP 5, 25, 125, ...
Find which term of the GP 2, 6, 18, ... is 4374, and write both the explicit formula and the recursive formula for the nth term.

Given a ball dropped from 80 metres that bounces back to 60% of its previous height each time, find (i) the height reached after the 5th bounce, and (ii) the total vertical distance travelled by the time it hits the ground for the 6th time.
Find which term of the sequence 2, 2sqrt(2), 4, ... equals 128.

For the Sierpinski square carpet (Stages 0-3, where each stage trisects the sides of each shaded square, removes the centre square, and retains the 8 outer squares): (i) count the red squares in Stages 0-3, (ii) predict the count at Stages 4 and 5, (iii) find the explicit and recursive formulas for the number of red squares at the nth stage, and (iv) given a Stage-0 area of 1 square unit, find the area of the red region at Stages 1-5 and the explicit and recursive formulas for the area at the nth stage, and describe what happens to this area as n increases.
Find the 31st term of an AP whose 11th term is 38 and 16th term is 73.
Determine the AP whose third term is 16 and whose 7th term exceeds the 5th term by 12.
